item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea, finding it intriguing and with a lot of possibility. But there are a number of problems with how it's written.

First, as has been pointed out, the whole "ownership" thing is unclear. It wasn't until I had read over it a couple times that I understood that it was referring to the person storing the memory. Also, how were they able to determine that level of specificity about what "owner" means without reaching that level of understanding in other areas?

This is clearly NOT a "safe" object. It's able to be activated from a distance, accidentally. It can alter reality retroactively. And the limits of what can be altered by storing memories is unclear. One person accidentally shoving memories around containment procedures or even research on other SCPs could conceivably lead to a retroactive containment breach. Because of all this complexity and possibility, it feels like the write-up really undersells the power, potential, and danger of this object.

Also, the revised containment procedures don't feel like they really fit. I understand why they'd switch to a group being put in charge. But the whole "unanimous" thing doesn't seem justified or all that relevant. I'm not certain what it's trying to accomplish or prevent - if something is "forgotten" via the jar in a manner that has a big enough impact to affect the entire group of researchers, a documented dissenting opinion likely wouldn't offer much of a counterpoint.

I had originally intended to make one point about why I was doing a neutral vote, but kept finding more to point out and convinced myself to downvote. I'd love to see a rewrite that really explores the potential of the jar.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well Done! I'll give it an upvote.

item.title: Rewrite Team Captain Post
item.content:encoded: The article is sitting at +29 right now (removing removed members) so any rewrite you would like to make will first require original author Abhainn's permission.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is the original author still around? I think I could improve some things with a rewrite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I can improve the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a little bland, but I liked it enough to upvote. I think you need to be clearer about how ideas are removed, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's O5, not 05. But don't worry, I fixed it per te.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like a lot of this, but there are several places where it's difficult to figure out the intended meaning. For example, in this sentence:

However, the holderowning entity2 (see following for details) may store concepts or ideas into the jar through a poorly understood process.

it is hard to tell if you mean the owner of the jar or the owner of the concept. In fact, since people generally don't even think of concepts and ideas as own-able, most people will probably assume you mean owner of the jar. I do think it's worth putting the effort in, so I suggest getting someone to help you edit for clarity (because it's really hard to spot clarity problems in our own work).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty much how I feel. That was enough for me in chat, and it's enough for me now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I uh… no idea what you're talking about.

(fixed, thank you, no idea how I missed that)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Test E-2103-T1 - Date

Are these headers supposed to contain actual dates, not just the word, "date"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wish it had gone into the entity that resisted Amnestic treatment. Ithink that's more interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea is bland, but it's well written so I'll give it an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a basic thing that does a thing, but the thing it is and the thing it does are weird enough to earn my upvote.
